title: Decode Ways
description:
An old cipher maps digits to letters: "1" through "26" spell out A through Z, and a message is encoded by concatenating those numbers with no separators between them. Given the encoded digit string `s`, count how many distinct ways it could have been decoded back into letters. A `'0'` can never stand on its own (there is no letter numbered 0) -- it only makes sense as the second digit of "10" or "20".

**Example**

```
num_decodings("12")   -> 2   ("AB" or "L")
num_decodings("226")  -> 3   ("BZ", "VF", "BBF")
num_decodings("06")   -> 0   (a leading zero has no valid decoding at all)
```
hints: Let dp[i] be the number of ways to decode the first i characters of s; dp[0] = 1 (the empty prefix decodes one way).
dp[i] adds dp[i-1] whenever s[i-1] is not '0' (a valid single-digit letter), and adds dp[i-2] whenever the two-digit number s[i-2:i] is between 10 and 26 inclusive.
A '0' contributes nothing on its own -- it can only ever be absorbed as the second digit of a valid two-digit group like "10" or "20".

solutions.name: Brute-force recursion over split points
solutions.explanation: From each position, recursively try consuming one digit (if it isn't '0') and try consuming two digits (if that two-digit number is 10-26), summing the ways both branches complete the rest of the string. With no memoization this revisits the same positions repeatedly.
solutions.complexity: Time O(2^n), Space O(n) recursion depth

solutions.name: Bottom-up DP with rolling variables
solutions.explanation: Track dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] as two running variables while scanning left to right. At each position, add the one-digit contribution if the current character isn't '0', and add the two-digit contribution if the previous and current characters form a number from 10 to 26. This avoids recomputation and uses constant extra space.
